package models

import "time"

type ContractStatus string

const (
	ContractStatusPendingEligibility  ContractStatus = "PENDING_ELIGIBILITY"
	ContractStatusEligibilityApproved ContractStatus = "ELIGIBILITY_APPROVED"
	ContractStatusApproved            ContractStatus = "APPROVED"
	ContractStatusDeclined            ContractStatus = "DECLINED"
	ContractStatusHandsetOrdered      ContractStatus = "HANDSET_ORDERED"
)

type Contract struct {
	ID                string         `bson:"id"`
	ServiceID         string         `bson:"serviceId"`
	CustomerID        string         `bson:"customerId"`
	HandsetID         string         `bson:"handsetId"`
	HandsetName       string         `bson:"handsetName"`
	ContractPrice     string         `bson:"contractPrice"`
	RetailPrice       string         `bson:"retailPrice"`
	ContractMonths    string         `bson:"contractMonths"`
	RequestID         string         `bson:"requestId"`
	LoanReference     string         `bson:"loanReference,omitempty"`
	BankAccountNumber string         `bson:"bankAccountNumber,omitempty"`
	BankTitle         string         `bson:"bankTitle,omitempty"`
	Status            ContractStatus `bson:"status"`
	CreatedAt         time.Time      `bson:"createdAt"`
	UpdatedAt         time.Time      `bson:"updatedAt"`
}

content.text:
Fair. Here's the core mental model:

An index in MongoDB is a sorted copy of one or more fields, stored separately. When a query matches an indexed field, Mongo walks the index instead of scanning every document.

**The tradeoff is always: faster reads vs. slower writes + more storage.** Every write must also update all indexes on that collection.

For compound indexes specifically, order matters. An index on `(customerId, status)` can serve:
- queries filtering by `customerId` alone
- queries filtering by `customerId + status`

But NOT queries filtering by `status` alone. The leftmost prefix rule.

**For our case:** a customer will have very few contracts (1-2 realistically). So even without the index, Mongo would only scan a tiny number of docs after matching `customerId`. The index on `customerId` is genuinely useful. Adding `status` as the second field is a minor optimisation. `createdAt` as third adds almost no value given the small result set.

My suggestion: drop `createdAt` from the index, keep `(customerId, status)`.

Want me to make that change?

content.text:
Based on my search of the repository, here's what I found about the local development workflow:

## Local Development Workflow Summary

**Primary Setup & Execution:**
1. **Start Environment**: Use `make start-environment` (Makefile target at root) to spin up Docker Compose dependencies
   - This runs: `docker-compose -f ./docker/docker-compose.yaml --profile dev up`

file.content:
FROM golang:1.26 AS build-stage

ARG services
ENV GOPRIVATE=github.com/*

WORKDIR /app

RUN --mount=type=secret,id=gh_auth_token,env=TOKEN \
    git config --global url."https://${TOKEN}@github.com/".insteadOf "https://github.com/"

# install binaries, these will rarely change and so can be defined here rather than modules.yaml
RUN go install github.com/utilitywarehouse/protoc-gen-protostrate@v0.2.3
RUN go install github.com/bufbuild/buf/cmd/buf@v1.54.0

# add buf dir and generate dependencies
ADD buf/src .
ADD buf/modules.yaml .
ADD buf/buf.lock .
RUN go run main.go

# print buf.yaml generated from modules.yaml
RUN cat buf.yaml

# clone git dependencies generated from modules.yaml
RUN sh deps.sh

# run
#ADD protos protos/github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile/protos
ADD protos protos
RUN cat buf.yaml
RUN buf dep update
RUN buf generate
RUN bash swagger.sh \
    "gen/openapi/mobile/v1/apis" \
    "$services" \
    "/app/swagger-gen"

FROM scratch AS export-stage
COPY --from=build-stage /app/gen/go/github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile /
COPY --from=build-stage /app/gen/openapi/mobile/v1/apis /services

# Copy generated swagger over services directory (directory structure is the same).
COPY --from=build-stage /app/swagger-gen/services /services
COPY --from=build-stage /app/buf.yaml /buf/buf.yaml
COPY --from=build-stage /app/buf.lock /buf/buf.lock

content.text:
The token is mounted but the `go install` on line 12 runs *after* the git config on line 8-9 — they're separate `RUN` layers so the git config doesn't persist. That's a bug in the Dockerfile, but not yours to fix.

The token itself: `gh auth token` gives an OAuth token, but private repo access via HTTPS needs a classic PAT with `repo` scope. Check what your token is:

```bash
gh auth status
```

If it shows `oauth_token` rather than a PAT, generate a classic PAT at GitHub → Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Tokens (classic), with `repo` scope, then:

```bash
export GH_AUTH_TOKEN=<your-pat>
make buf-gen
```

FROM golang:1.26 AS build-stage

ARG services
ENV GOPRIVATE=github.com/*

WORKDIR /app

RUN --mount=type=secret,id=gh_auth_token,env=TOKEN \
    git config --global url."https://${TOKEN}@github.com/".insteadOf "https://github.com/"

# install binaries, these will rarely change and so can be defined here rather than modules.yaml
RUN go install github.com/utilitywarehouse/protoc-gen-protostrate@v0.2.3
RUN go install github.com/bufbuild/buf/cmd/buf@v1.54.0

# add buf dir and generate dependencies
ADD buf/src .
ADD buf/modules.yaml .
ADD buf/buf.lock .
RUN go run main.go

# print buf.yaml generated from modules.yaml
RUN cat buf.yaml

# clone git dependencies generated from modules.yaml
RUN sh deps.sh

# run
#ADD protos protos/github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile/protos
ADD protos protos
RUN cat buf.yaml
RUN buf dep update
RUN buf generate
RUN bash swagger.sh \
    "gen/openapi/mobile/v1/apis" \
    "$services" \
    "/app/swagger-gen"

FROM scratch AS export-stage
COPY --from=build-stage /app/gen/go/github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile /
COPY --from=build-stage /app/gen/openapi/mobile/v1/apis /services

# Copy generated swagger over services directory (directory structure is the same).
COPY --from=build-stage /app/swagger-gen/services /services
COPY --from=build-stage /app/buf.yaml /buf/buf.yaml
COPY --from=build-stage /app/buf.lock /buf/buf.lock

1	package eligibility
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"context"
6		"encoding/json"
7		"fmt"
8		"io"
9		"net/http"
10		"time"
11	
12		"github.com/utilitywarehouse/uwos-go/telemetry/tracing"
13		"github.com/utilitywarehouse/uwos-go/telemetry/uwconv"
14		"go.opentelemetry.io/otel/attribute"
15		"go.opentelemetry.io/otel/trace"
16	
17		"github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile/pkg/handseteligibility"
18		"github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile/pkg/libdry/tlog"
19	)
20	
21	// Client is an HTTP client for the third-party eligibility provider.
22	type Client struct {
23		httpClient *http.Client
24		baseURL    string
25		authToken  string
26	}
27	
28	// NewClient creates a new eligibility client.
29	func NewClient(baseURL, authToken string, timeout time.Duration) *Client {
30		return &Client{
31			httpClient: &http.Client{
32				Timeout: timeout,
33			},
34			baseURL:   baseURL,
35			authToken: authToken,
36		}
37	}
38	
39	// RequestEligibilityCheck sends an eligibility check request to the third-party provider.
40	// This is called asynchronously after contract creation — the provider will respond
41	// via the EligibilityCallback endpoint.
42	func (c *Client) RequestEligibilityCheck(ctx context.Context, req handseteligibility.EligibilityRequest) error {
43		ctx, span := tracing.Tracer().Start(ctx, "EligibilityCheck", trace.WithAttributes(
44			attribute.String("request_id", req.RequestID),
45			attribute.String("handset_id", req.HandsetID),
46			uwconv.CustomerAccountID(req.CustomerID),
47		))
48		defer span.End()
49	
50		body, err := json.Marshal(req)
51		if err != nil {
52			_ = tracing.RecordSpanError(span, err)
53			return fmt.Errorf("failed to marshal eligibility request: %w", err)
54		}
55	
56		url := fmt.Sprintf("%s/eligibility/check", c.baseURL)
57		httpReq, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodPost, url, bytes.NewReader(body))
58		if err != nil {
59			_ = tracing.RecordSpanError(span, err)
60			return fmt.Errorf("failed to create eligibility request: %w", err)
61		}
62	
63		httpReq.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
64		if c.authToken != "" {
65			httpReq.Header.Set("Authorization", fmt.Sprintf("Bearer %s", c.authToken))
66		}
67	
68		resp, err := c.httpClient.Do(httpReq)
69		if err != nil {
70			_ = tracing.RecordSpanError(span, err)
71			return fmt.Errorf("failed to send eligibility request: %w", err)
72		}
73		defer func() {
74			if closeErr := resp.Body.Close(); closeErr != nil {
75				tlog.Error(ctx, "failed to close eligibility response body: %v", closeErr)
76			}
77		}()
78	
79		span.SetAttributes(attribute.Int("http.status_code", resp.StatusCode))
80	
81		if resp.StatusCode < 200 || resp.StatusCode >= 300 {
82			respBody := "none"
83			if rb, err := io.ReadAll(resp.Body); err == nil {
84				respBody = string(rb)
85			}
86			respErr := fmt.Errorf("eligibility provider returned status %d: %s", resp.StatusCode, respBody)
87			_ = tracing.RecordSpanError(span, respErr)
88			return respErr
89		}
90	
91		return nil
92	}
93

content.text:
It's a plain HTTP client hitting a third-party URL. There's no local mock server or stub for it — `ELIGIBILITY_PROVIDER_URL` just points at the real provider in all environments.

For local dev you'd either point it at a local HTTP stub you run yourself, or just set it to any valid `https://` URL and let it fail gracefully since the eligibility check is async (fire-and-forget — the real response comes back via the callback endpoint).

content.text:
No seed data. The handset collection gets populated by `handsetLoader` — it runs at startup and pulls handset catalogue data from EquinoxDB (the `EQUINOXDB_CONN_STR`). Contracts are created at runtime via the API.

So for local dev you'd need a real or stubbed EquinoxDB connection to get handsets, then create contracts via API calls. There's no pre-seeded test data.
